ach_question: Where does the document primarily locate responsibility or violation theory?

hypotheses.label: Batch microbial testing failure
hypotheses.claim: The warning letter primarily locates responsibility in failing to test each batch for objectionable microorganisms before release.

hypotheses.label: Quality unit data integrity failure
hypotheses.claim: The warning letter primarily locates responsibility in quality-unit oversight and data-integrity failures.

hypotheses.label: Stability or expiration dating failure
hypotheses.claim: The warning letter primarily locates responsibility in stability testing or expiration dating failures.

hypotheses.label: Border refusal outcome only
hypotheses.claim: The core agency theory is simply that one shipment was refused at the border, not that a manufacturing system violated CGMP.

evidence_rows.label: Regulation and failure to test each batch
evidence_rows.text_anchor: failed to conduct, for each batch of drug product, appropriate laboratory testing
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct support for h1 and anchors the first enumerated violation.

evidence_rows.label: Products released without microbial testing
evidence_rows.text_anchor: without adequate testing for critical microbial attributes
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct support for h1; strengthens the public-health consequence.

evidence_rows.label: Sampling some batches instead of every batch
evidence_rows.text_anchor: Rather than testing every batch, you conducted (b)(4) tests on some of your batches
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct support for h1 and distinguishes systematic release testing from mere paperwork.

evidence_rows.label: FDA contamination finding at border
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Pivotal direct support for h1: it supplies the single concrete hook showing why the missing microbial testing matters.

evidence_rows.label: No scientific evidence all batches were free of contamination
evidence_rows.text_anchor: you did not have scientific evidence that all (b)(4) drug product batches were free of objectionable microbial contamination
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct support for h1 and links testing omission to lack of batch assurance.

evidence_rows.label: No retain-sample commitment
evidence_rows.text_anchor: did not commit to testing your retain samples
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Partial support for h1; also supports ongoing remediation inadequacy.

evidence_rows.label: QU data reliability issue
evidence_rows.text_anchor: quality unit (QU) failed to provide adequate oversight and to ensure the reliability of data
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Direct support for h2, a plausible but secondary responsibility theory.

evidence_rows.label: Consultant recommendation
evidence_rows.text_anchor: should engage a consultant qualified as set forth in 21 CFR 211.34
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Partial support for systemic CGMP remediation; not specific enough to make h2 or h3 win.

expected_read.rationale: The first and most concrete violation is failure to test every batch for objectionable microorganisms, reinforced by FDA's contamination finding. Data integrity is important but presented as a second violation.
expected_read.flip_note: Removing e4 removes the single concrete contamination hook and should make h1 much less dominant, allowing h2 to look more competitive even if h1 can still be supported by e1-e3 and e5.
